turns.content: What's the difference between Iterator and DoubleEndedIterator?

Good question! `Iterator` is a trait that allows you to traverse elements from front-to-back with a `next()` method. On the other hand, `DoubleEndedIterator` extends this by adding a `next_back()` method, enabling traversal from back-to-front as well.
So if I implement DoubleEndedIterator, can I just call next() and then next_back()?
Yes, but implementing both is necessary. Implementing `next()` alone won't make your iterator double-ended.
How do I implement DoubleEndedIterator with next_back()?

You can implement `DoubleEndedIterator` by defining a custom struct that holds the data structure you want to iterate over. Then, in your implementation of `next_back()`, traverse from the end towards the beginning.
But what if I just want to reverse an existing iterator? Can't I use rev()?
Absolutely! `rev()` is a convenience adapter that takes any iterator and returns a new one that iterates in reverse order. It's implemented for many types, like vectors or slices.
So if I have a vector, can I do something like this?
Yes! You can call `vec.iter().rev()` to get an iterator that goes from the last element to the first.
What are some std iterators that implement DoubleEndedIterator?
`Vec<T>`, `String`, and slices all implement `DoubleEndedIterator`. You can reverse these directly with `.iter().rev()` or `.into_iter().rev()`, depending on ownership.
Can I use rev() with take() to get the last N elements?
Absolutely! Use `iter().rev().take(n)` to get the last `n` elements efficiently. This is one of the neat things about iterator adaptors.
Is there any downside to using rev() with take()?
No, but be careful if you reverse a mutable iterator. You might need to clone the data first.
Hey, I've read about the DoubleEndedIterator trait but I still don't really get how it actually works in practice. Like, what's the difference between just an iterator and a double-ended one?

Think of a normal iterator like walking through a book from front to back; you can only go forward with next(). A DoubleEndedIterator is like having two readers, one starting at the beginning and another starting at the end, so you can walk both ways using next() and prev() methods. This lets you traverse a collection in either direction without needing separate data structures.
That makes sense for traversal, but what about when I need to actually implement this? Do I just add the trait to any iterator?

You can't just add it anywhere; you have to explicitly implement DoubleEndedIterator for your custom type. The trait requires both next() and prev() methods, so your collection needs to support bidirectional access or be backed by something like a Vec that allows random access. Standard iterators like vec::into_iter are single-ended unless you wrap them in something else.
Okay, but what if I just want the last N elements without reversing everything? Is there a simpler way than implementing my own DoubleEndedIterator?

You can use the rev() adapter which flips your iterator so next() now returns items from back to front. Then combine that with take() to grab exactly N elements from the end without needing a full bidirectional implementation on your own type.
So I'd call rev() then take()? But wait, does this still require next_back or something specific?

No, you don't need next_back for that pattern. The rev() adapter just swaps the direction so your existing single-ended iterator now behaves like it's going backwards, and take() simply counts how many items to pull from that reversed stream.
